case_name: Constitutional Court of Russia on the Death Penalty Moratorium
court: Constitutional Court of the Russian Federation
citation: Decision of the Constitutional Court of the Russian Federation, No. 1344-O-R (19 November 2009)
text: DEATH PENALTY MORATORIUM DECISION (2009). COURT: Constitutional Court of the Russian Federation. The Constitutional Court ruled that the moratorium on the death penalty in Russia, which had been in effect since 1996 as a condition of Russia's accession to the Council of Europe, had acquired constitutional significance. Although Russia signed but never ratified Protocol No. 6 to the ECHR (abolition of the death penalty in peacetime), the Court held that the moratorium had created a legitimate expectation and a guaranteed right to life that could not be reversed. The Court stated that the death penalty could not be applied in Russia, effectively establishing a permanent constitutional prohibition even without formal ratification of the Protocol. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Effectively abolished the death penalty in Russia through constitutional interpretation. (2) Demonstrated the influence of ECHR obligations on Russian constitutional law. (3) Key example of the interaction between international human rights commitments and domestic constitutional law.

citation: OAO Neftyanaya Kompaniya Yukos v Russia, Application No. 14902/04, ECHR (2011)
text: YUKOS v RUSSIA (ECHR, 2011). COURT: European Court of Human Rights. FACTS: OAO Neftyanaya Kompaniya Yukos, a major Russian oil company, alleged that its rights under the European Convention on Human Rights had been violated through politically motivated tax assessments, enforcement measures, and ultimately the company's forced liquidation. HOLDING: The ECHR found violations of Article 1 of Protocol No. 1 (protection of property) and Article 6 (right to a fair trial). The Court found that the enforcement proceedings against Yukos involved disproportionate measures. The Grand Chamber later awarded over €1.86 billion in damages (the largest award in ECHR history). SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Landmark in the PROTECTION OF PROPERTY rights under the ECHR. (2) Largest damages award in ECHR history. (3) Significant case on the relationship between state power, taxation, and human rights. (4) Key precedent on politically motivated prosecutions.

court: European Court of Human Rights (Grand Chamber)
citation: Konstantin Markin v Russia, Application No. 30078/06, ECHR Grand Chamber (2012)
text: MARKIN v RUSSIA (2012). COURT: European Court of Human Rights (Grand Chamber). FACTS: Konstantin Markin, a Russian military serviceman, was denied parental leave to care for his children after his divorce. Russian law granted three years of parental leave to female military personnel but not to male personnel. He challenged this as gender discrimination. HOLDING: The Grand Chamber found a violation of Article 14 (prohibition of discrimination) taken together with Article 8 (right to respect for private and family life). The difference in treatment between men and women in the armed forces regarding parental leave had no reasonable and objective justification. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Landmark on GENDER EQUALITY in parental leave. (2) Important precedent on non-discrimination in military service. (3) Demonstrated the application of ECHR standards to Russian military law.

case_name: Kudla v Poland — Application to Russian Cases
citation: Kudla v Poland, Application No. 30210/96, ECHR Grand Chamber (2000)
text: KUDLA v POLAND (2000) — frequently applied in Russian cases. COURT: European Court of Human Rights (Grand Chamber). The Grand Chamber established that Article 13 ECHR (right to an effective remedy) requires that domestic legal systems provide an effective remedy for violations of the Convention, including for complaints about the length of judicial proceedings under Article 6. This case prompted Russia and other Council of Europe states to create domestic mechanisms for addressing unreasonable delays in judicial proceedings. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established the requirement of EFFECTIVE DOMESTIC REMEDIES for Convention violations. (2) Led to systematic reforms in Russian judicial procedure regarding trial delays. (3) Key case on the interaction between Articles 6 and 13 ECHR.
